speaker: interviewer
text: Sonnenschein Tape number five

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sonnenschein, when you came to Vancouver, you had your happiness, and you had your tragedy. But you pick up-- picked up your pieces, and you kept on going, and still going strong.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter, she wanted you to stop working.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son too, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Together, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you have a message that you wanted for world to hear. I understand that you are doing a lot of community work. You're going to schools, educational centers, and you're telling the story what happened. Can you tell us in short, which organization you have been working with, schools that you been going through, or awards that you won?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Let me just tell you, that for 40 years, I was silent. I could not speak about it. I couldn't face the-- speaking in public. And it was much too painful to speak about it. I was encouraged by my children, by friends, Dr. Krell, and other people. And I came out and started to speak. And I haven't stopped, in I guess, eight or nine years now. And I know I'm not going to change the world. But I know in my heart that I have made a dent, that I have made the children that I talked to, young children, students in high school, students at the university, many grownups, the Christian faith, I have made them see what has been done to my people, the Jewish people, And I have derived the greatest satisfaction when I see their understanding, their compassion. And it gives me so much strength, that even one person can do something. And so with God's help, I will continue what I'm doing. I have received, in June, June 7, 1995, a beautiful award from the Vancouver Holocaust Center Society, for Outstanding Contribution to Holocaust Education. I'm very proud and very happy about it. I received an award from students, 700 that I addressed in Kelowna, in Ontario, of BC, thanking me. And when I was in Simon-- I am now twice, I will be going again-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was given, it was a program of racism and anti-Semitism. I was on the same program like the natives, First Nation. And I got to know some of them. And one woman, when I was there last year, gave me an Indian, an eagle's feather. It is the greatest honor a native woman can give you, by anyone. And she-- on that feather there is a stem, three colors. So she explained to me what the colors mean, what she has seen in me. I will show them the feather. This is my eagle's feather. These are the colors of the stem that she explained. The orange one is for compassion. The light yellow one is for the inner light. The purple one is for knowledge. That, what she has seen in me. | value this feather very highly. It was given also with great love, and great understanding. We all have problems. The natives have their problems. We had ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sonnenschein, I understand that your son is here, and he will join us in a few minutes. So we'll stop right now after he comes, and then if you will give us your last message, what you wanted to send to the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now?

speaker: interviewer
text: When your son comes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, when he's here? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sonnenschein, you have a gentleman next to you. Would just tell us, who is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: This my son, Dan, and the best friend in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dan, do you have a message to tell the world, about your mom, her experiences what you heard, and whatever you have and want to say? This is few minutes of yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Well, I don't really have a prepared message for the world. I'll leave that to my mother. I want to express how proud I am of my mother. As we say in Yiddish, [YIDDISH], from her. I have tremendous respect for the wonderful work she's doing. It's very courageous of her. I know it's difficult for her every time. Although she's done it many times, it's difficult each time. She's a wonderful communicator. She is able to reach people. I've seen the wonderful comments she's had from all types of people, all ages, very young kids as well as older adults. And she's changed some people's lives. I know she's reached some people, made tremendous impressions on them. And I appreciate her influence as a mother, and her building of a strong family, creating a strong Jewish identity for her children. And now her grandchildren are being brought up in a strong Jewish faith, or in a belief and continuation of their heritage and tradition.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think it's a tremendous triumph that my mother represents over-- over Nazism. And I'm very grateful and proud of her. Mrs. Sonnenschein, would you like to give us your last words, the message you want to convey to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is for my children, for my grandchildren, for all the children. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Not all of us have been given a special talent, a gift. But all of us can try to be the best in whatever we do, by being tolerant and understanding towards each other, regardless of the religion we practice, or the color of our skin. We can, and we must make a commitment towards world peace. Do not expect to do others, your job. You have to stand on your own feet, and never give up. There are bound to be stones along the way. Try to step over them, and meet the next challenge. I believe in miracles. I survived against all odds. Believe in miracles. They do come true sometimes. Good luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Sonnenschein. Thank you for giving such a wonderful testimony. So it's going to be recorded and it's going to be for the generation to come, to see what people went through, and how they can prevent that the history does not repeat itself. I understand you have some pictures and some documentations that is going to be taken now, photographed. And if you will, please--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I end my presentation, may I read a very short thank you to the creator of the Shoah project?

speaker: interviewer
text: By all means, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is special thank you to Steven Spielberg, the creator of the Shoah project. It is not a monument carved in stone and marble. It is the voice of survivors. They miraculously survived the horrors of the Holocaust. It is the message of survivors directed to future generations, lest they forget what has been done to the Jewish people. May you, your children, all your loved ones, be spared the cruelties of hatred, unprovoked, causeless, hatred. Thank you, Steven Spielberg. Shalom. This is called, schutzbrief. It means a letter that should protect you, or will protect you. It was given to me by a German woman on the day we left for The Death March. It was given to me in good faith, and without knowing me, or my name. She knew my face, from working there. She for some reason, wanted me to live. She had to watch, not to be seen by the guards, because she would have had a problem. I would have had a bigger problem. So she made sure nobody sees it. And she thrust the letter into my hand. And she said, take this letter. It will protect you if you believe in it-- which I did. This is the letter. It says on top, Haus and Schutzbrief, home and letter of protection. It's in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost, Amen. And there was letters, L, J, F, K, E, B, K, N, K. I do not know what the letters are. But it says this are the letters of the-- of [NON-ENGLISH], of help. And then it says that in this letter, there is help in it. And whoever has this [NON-ENGLISH] letter with him, nothing bad will come-- no bullets, no fire, no water, no enemy attack. And it says, mentions a few of the Ten Commandments. But over and over, it stresses the fact, unless you believe in it, it will not help you. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This letter was given to me by a woman, by the name of Ubschat, U-B-S-C-H-A-T. I knew her name. She didn't know mine. The letter further stresses that the letter was written the year 1700, found in Germany. And it suggested it should be given, passed on to other people, whom we want to be helped. And it cites an example that it was put around a dog. The dog was shot at, but did not get killed. It's just an example. The first name was Abraham. The last name was Schwebel. He was my father. I don't know which country, whether it was Austria or still in Poland. I do not know. It doesn't say. The year, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. It was 1900 maybe. It could be, but I really cannot say for sure. The first name was Emilia, last name, Kawalek. She was my mother. It was taken, I believe still in Galicia The year, again, she must have been about 17 years old. I don't not quite know the year to pinpoint. She was born in 1892, so it was-- I really don't know the date. These are my parents, Emilia-- she was still Kawalek-- and Abraham Schwebel. I think they were engaged at the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe when-- it was taken-- oh, that was still taken in Husiatyn, in Galicia And the date must have been-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was in March was their wedding day. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know. I really don't know. My mother, Emilia-- by then she was Schwebel. A young woman. She was a giant. I'm not sure when it was taken, so this was probably also still either in Vienna, or still in Husiatyn, and maybe 1912, I would say, approximately. These are my maternal grandparents, Phillip Kawalek, Cipora Kawalek, his wife, my grandmother. They were both taken in Vienna, Austria. And again, I really do not know the year. My grandfather died in Milan, Italy. He went there on a cure. He had heart problems. He was 54 when he died. So that was obviously taken before. This is my favorite picture. It is my mother standing up, my father sitting down, Paula, who was three years younger on his lap, because she was scared. And I seem to look quite confident. It was obviously taken in Vienna. And maybe it was 1920. I really, again, I do not know. '20, '21, I do not know, around that time. With the a picture, I was Bronia Schwebel, there. I was six years old. It was at my first school day. Everybody in Vienna had their child's picture taken on their first day at school. Next to me is Paula. Her picture was taken later, but also on the first day she went to school. It was taken in Vienna. The year was around '24, '25, something around that time. On the left is my sister, Paula Schwebel. Sitting down is my mother, Emilia Schwebel. Standing behind her is me, Bronia Schwebel. And it was taken in Vienna, 1938. On the left, is me, Bronia Sonnenschein, and my sister Paula Langer. It was taken in Vienna, probably 1935, maybe. Left is Ignatz Yellen. Next to him is Nubright Mary Schifflinger, the one I refer to. This was Chaim Rumkowski, blessing us. Next to Mary, Bronia Schwebel. And next to me, my husband Erich Straus. It was taken September 18, 1943, in the Lodz ghetto. This is our wedding picture, September 18, 1943, taken in the Lodz ghetto. If you look close enough, you can see my husband wearing the Star of David. Jude-- Jew. On the left is Chaim Rumkowski, the Jewish administrator of the Lodz ghetto. Next to him is the German administrator, Hans Biebow. We are not aware when the picture was taken. As you can see on Rumkowski's shoulder, is also the Star of David, Jew on it. That's me, here. three months-- July 1945, three months after liberation. In the middle is my mother. And next to my mother is Paula. We needed it for identification papers. The photo was taken in Vienna, in July 1945. I'm on the left side. This is a ghetto mark, bearing the signature of Chaim Rumkowski. We were paid in ghetto mark, where we could buy our food ration, nothing else. It was totally worthless money. It was only in the ghetto, to buy your ration carte. It says [NON-ENGLISH]. It is the equivalent for two mark. The eldest of the Jews in Litzmannstadt, signature, Chaim Rumkowski. The date is May 15, 1940. This is Bronia Straus, I was then, because it was taken 1947, well before I was married, in Prague-- former Czechoslovakia. This was our wedding picture, Kurt Sonnenschein, Bronia Sonnenschein. This was taken in Prague, Czechoslovakia, December 28, 1948. This was my mother, Emilia in Canada, Emily Schwebel. It was taken in our kitchen, in Vancouver, 1973, 1975, two months before my daughter got married. She died in 1975. This was my sister, Paula Langer. It was taken in Vancouver, in her living room. The year was 1984. We had just returned from a trip to Italy, where she bought the doll that she named Giovanni. This is my pride and joy, my children, Vivian, on the left, Dan, my son, on the right. It was taken in Calgary, Alberta, in August 1995. This is my son-- my son-in-law, Ben. Next to him in the middle, the bar mitzvah boy, my grandson, Jonathan. Next to him on the right, Vivian Herman, my daughter. It was taken in Canada, on August 12th, 1995. On the left is Jonathan. It was taken in Calgary, Canada, August 1995. In the middle is Claire. She is 15 years old. She's my granddaughter. Next to her sitting down on the right, is Emily, who is 17. She's my oldest granddaughter. It was all taken in Calgary, Canada, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I refer to the letter? This is the letter in reply to a letter that I wrote to Dr. Viktor Frankl, from Vancouver to Vienna, where he resided, where he still resides. And it was-- I read the book in the late '60s, when my son took a course-- studied psychology.

speaker: interviewee
text: Reading the book, explained to me so much what had happened to me. I thanked him from all my heart for having opened my mind, for having made me understand. And the quote that he mentioned in his book, it was a quote by Friedrich Nietzsche, a German philosopher who said once, 'If you have a why to live, you can bear with almost any how.' I realize I had the best reason to live-- for my mother, and for my sister. That was my reason to live. And Dr. Frankl made me understand it. This is an award given to me from the Vancouver Holocaust Center Society for Education and Remembrance. In recognition of Outstanding Contribution to Holocaust Education, appoints Bronia Sonnenschein, a life fellow of the society. Signed, Robert Krell, Elie Wiesel, June 7, 1995, Vancouver, Canada.
